coredata.dc:title: Coordination-induced self-assembly based carbon dot dendrimers as efficient signal labels for electrochemiluminescent immunosensor construction
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Zi
Cheng, Wenqian
Liu, Changjin
Zhao, Min
Ding, Shijia
Deng, Zhongliang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an “on-off” electrochemiluminescent immunosensor for bone alkaline phosphatase (BALP) detection was constructed based on the carbon dot dendrimers (CD DRs) as signal labels and Pt nanoparticles functionalized Ni-phenolic coordination spheres (Pt@Ni-PCS) as quenching labels. The prepared CD DRs with low cytotoxicity were synthesized via coordination-induced self-assembly of carboxyl groups-rich N-doped carbon dots and zirconium oxygen clusters, of which the ECL efficiency was 2-fold enhancement than that of discrete N-doped carbon dots. Benefiting from the efficient quenching effect of Pt@Ni-PCS toward Zr-CD DRs/triethylamine-based ECL system, the prepared immunoassay for BALP detection exhibited a broad linear range with 1 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL and a low limit of detection of 24.9 fg/mL. Importantly, this highlights coordination-induced self-assembly as a tool for carbon dots enrichment to construct efficient ECL nanocomposites, which conceptualizes a pathway to expand the application of carbon dots in clinical ECL analysis technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Electrochemiluminescence
Coordination-induced self-assembly
Immunosensor
Bone alkaline phosphatase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Pt nanoparticles functionalized Ni-phenolic coordination spheres (Pt@Ni-PCS)
Cytotoxicity of CDs and Zr-CD DRs
ECL quantum efficiency of Zr-CD DRs
ECL and EIS characterization of the immunosensor
Analytical performance of immunosensor for BALP detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 82272431 , 21804015 ), the Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC ( cstc2018jcyjAX0206 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic diagram of CDs synthesis, (B) HRTEM image of CDs, (C) AFM image of CDs and the height profile of along the white line, (D) Fluorescence excitation spectrum (blue line) and emission spectrum (red line) of CDs dispersed in deionized water at room temperature. The inset of D: photographs of CDs solution under the radiation of daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) SEM image of Zr-CD DRs, the inset of A: the local enlarged Zr-CD DRs with the scale bars of 500 nm, (B) TEM image of Zr-CD DRs, (C) the high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF)-STEM image of Zr-CD DRs. (D–H) STEM-EDS elemental mapping of Zr-CD DRs: C, N, O, Zr.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of ZrOCl2, CDs and Zr-CD DRs, the inset of A: the local enlarged of the blue dotted line. (B) XRD patterns of CDs and Zr-CD DRs. (C) XPS survey spectra of Zr-CD DRs. XPS high resolution spectra of (D) C1s, (E) N1s, (F) O1s, and (G) Zr3d. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

TEM images of Ni-PCS (A), Pt@Ni-PCS (B) and the local enlarged Pt@Ni-PCS (C). The high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) STEM image of Pt@Ni-PCS (D) and corresponding element mapping images for Pt@Ni-PCS (E–I).
Viability of MDA-MB-468 cells after incubated with different concentrations of CDs and Zr-CD DRs for 48 h.
Calibration curves for the relationship between the ECL intensities and the currents of (A) CDs and (B) Zr-CD DRs.

(A) ECL intensity-time and (B) EIS curves of (a) bare GCE, (b) Zr-CD DRs/CS/GCE, (c) AgNPs/Zr-CD DRs/CS/GCE, (d) Ab1/AgNPs/Zr-CD DRs/CS/GCE, (e) BSA/Ab1/AgNPs/Zr-CD DRs/CS/GCE, (f) BALP/BSA/Ab1/AgNPs/Zr-CD DRs/CS/GCE, (g) Ab2 bioconjugates/BALP/BSA/Ab1/AgNPs/Zr-CD DRs/CS/GCE.

(A) Relationship ECL intensity and the logarithm of the concentration of BALP. (B) ECL intensity-time curves of the immunosensor with different concentrations of BALP. BALP concentrations: (a) 1 pg/mL, (b) 10 pg/mL, (c) 100 pg/mL, (d) 1 ng/mL, (e) 10 ng/mL and (f) 50 ng/mL. (C) Specificity of the proposed ECL immunosensor against different non-targets: AFP (10 ng/mL), LN (10 ng/mL), PSA (10 ng/mL), HSA (10 ng/mL), BALP (1 ng/mL) and mixture (1 ng/mL BALP with 10 ng/mL interferences). Error bars: SD, n = 3. (D) Stability of ECL signal under continuous scanning for 10 cycles with 10 pg/mL and 10 ng/mL BALP detection, respectively.

Schematic illustration of an “on-off” ECL immunosensor based on Zr-CD DRs as signal labels and Pt@Ni-PCSs as quenchers for highly sensitive detection of BALP. (A) The preparation of Zr-CD DRs, (B) the preparation of Pt@Ni-PCS-Ab2 bioconjugates, and (C) the possible ECL reaction mechanism.

head.ce:title: Coordination-induced self-assembly based carbon dot dendrimers as efficient signal labels for electrochemiluminescent immunosensor construction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopedics, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing, 400010, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Clinical Laboratory Diagnostics (Ministry of Education), College of Laboratory Medicine, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing, 400016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Clinical Laboratory Diagnostics (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Clinical Laboratory Diagnostics (Ministry of Education), College of Laboratory Medicine, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Laboratory Medicine, The Fifth People's Hospital of Chongqing, Chongqing, 400062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Fifth People's Hospital of Chongqing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Laboratory Medicine, The Fifth People's Hospital of Chongqing, Chongqing, 400062, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an “on-off” electrochemiluminescent immunosensor for bone alkaline phosphatase (BALP) detection was constructed based on the carbon dot dendrimers (CD DRs) as signal labels and Pt nanoparticles functionalized Ni-phenolic coordination spheres (Pt@Ni-PCS) as quenching labels. The prepared CD DRs with low cytotoxicity were synthesized via coordination-induced self-assembly of carboxyl groups-rich N-doped carbon dots and zirconium oxygen clusters, of which the ECL efficiency was 2-fold enhancement than that of discrete N-doped carbon dots. Benefiting from the efficient quenching effect of Pt@Ni-PCS toward Zr-CD DRs/triethylamine-based ECL system, the prepared immunoassay for BALP detection exhibited a broad linear range with 1 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL and a low limit of detection of 24.9 fg/mL. Importantly, this highlights coordination-induced self-assembly as a tool for carbon dots enrichment to construct efficient ECL nanocomposites, which conceptualizes a pathway to expand the application of carbon dots in clinical ECL analysis technology.

An highly sensitive “on-off” ECL immunosensor based on Zr-CD DRs as the signal labels and Pt@Ni-PCSs as the quenchers for BALP detection.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Bone alkaline phosphatase (BALP) is secreted by osteoblasts, which mainly reflects the activity and function of osteoblasts and acts as an important indicator of bone formation [1]. Clinically, serum level of BALP has become a sensitive and specific biomarker for diagnosis of rickets and bone metabolic diseases [2,3]. At present, the main methods for BALP detection rely on enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and colorimetry. However, the above-mentioned methods for BALP detection possess some shortcomings of poor sensitivity, complex operation and harsh reaction conditions. Thus, developing a simple, enzyme-free and sensitive methodology for BALP detection is highly desired. Electrochemiluminescencce (ECL) immunoassay has become an advanced analytical tool in clinical laboratory for disease biomarkers detection due to its high sensitivity, low background and excellent specificity [4,5], which is an idealized technique for BALP detection to solve above-mentioned problems.

Since the ECL behavior was first reported in 2009 [6], carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging class of luminescent carbon nanomaterials, have attracted research interest in bioanalysis benefiting from their prominent advantages of low cost, good biocompatibility and easy accessibility [7,8]. In the process of CDs research, researchers have been committed to developing efficient and stable enrichment and immobilization of CDs to obtain CDs-centric nano-labels. However, the current approaches (e.g., electrostatic adsorption [9], covalent crosslinking [10] and organic polymers encapsulating [11]) are often restricted in ECL applications due to the inadequate enrichment capacity and limited electron transfer. Strategies aiming at efficiently enriching CDs and maintaining or even improving the ECL efficiency remain a central theme. Coordination-induced self-assembly has become one of the most attractive strategies to construct the bottom-up nanomaterials in the field of supramolecular chemistry [12]. Examples of such ensembles are established from organic molecules containing carboxyl groups and metal ions. Inspired by the above idea, carbon dot dendrimers (CD DRs) are synthesized through coordination-induced self-assembly of carboxyl groups-rich CDs and zirconium oxygen clusters, which has not yet been reported in the field of CDs researches.

In this work, carboxyl groups-rich N-doped CDs, which are synthesized by solvothermal method with citric acid and hydrazine as precursors, are enriched via coordination interaction between the zirconium oxygen cluster and carboxyl groups of CDs to form Zr-CD dendrimers (Zr-CD DRs) (Scheme 1 A), realizing efficient and stable enrichment of CDs. Surprisingly, the ECL efficiency of Zr-CD DRs is significantly improved compared to that of dispersed CDs, which is attributed that the zirconium oxygen cluster plays a key role as an isolation strip. Benefiting from the Zr-CD DRs as ECL signal labels as well as electrode modification materials, a highly sensitive “on-off” ECL immunosensor for BALP detection is constructed based on Pt nanoparticles functionalized Ni-phenolic coordination spheres (Pt@Ni-PCS) as quenchers. Specifically, the as-prepared Zr-CD DRs and Ag nanoparticles (Ag NPs) are successively assembled onto the electrode surface, and then decorate with the capture anti-BALP (Ab1) to fabricate the ECL immunosensor. With the help of triethylamine (TEA) co-reactant, the ECL immunosensor modified with Zr-CD DRs obtains a strong ECL signal (named as “signal on” state). In the presence of BALP, the quencher labels of Pt@Ni-PCS-Ab2 bioconjugates are introduction via sandwich immuno-reaction. Due to the effective quenching of Pt@Ni-PCS toward Zr-CD DRs/TEA system, a weak ECL signal is obtained (named as “signal off” state). Therefore, the ECL signal significantly decreases with the increasing concentration of BALP, realizing the highly sensitive detection of BALP. Meaningfully, this work engineers Zr-CD DRs with high ECL performance via the introduction of coordination-induced self-assembly technique for efficient CDs enrichment, which is expected to show great promise in ECL analysis.

CDs were prepared according to the reported literature [13]. 2.1 g of citric acid and 1.0 mL of hydrazine hydrate were successively added into 10 mL of DMF. Followed by ultrasonic dispersion, the above mixture was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave lined with Teflon and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The product of brown solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to collect the upper solution which was further dialyzed (1000 Da) against deionized water. After vacuum dried at 60 °C, CDs powder was obtained and then stored at 4 °C.

ZrOCl2·8H2O (30 mg) was dispersed in 10 mL of DMF containing 1 mg/mL as-prepared CDs. Subsequently, the mixture was heated at 90 °C for 5 h. Followed by centrifugating and washing with the help of deionized water, the Zr-CD DRs products were re-dispersed in deionized water.

The preparation of AgNPs was on the basis of the reported literature [14] with minor modifications, and the detailed preparation process was as follows: 3.4 mg AgNO3 was dispersed in 20 mL of cool deionized water (about 10 °C). Next, a mixture of 4.53 mg of NaBH4 and 24 mg of sodium citrate was dropped into the AgNO3 solution and then stirred for 30 min in an ice bath condition. Finally, the AgNPs solution was prepared and stored in a brown closed container at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Pt nanoparticles functionalized Ni-phenolic coordination spheres (Pt@Ni-PCS)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the Ni-phenolic coordination spheres (Ni-PCS) were synthesized by formaldehyde-assisted metal-ligand crosslinking method [15]. Specifically, 0.1 g F127 was dissolved in a mixture of ethanol (4 mL) and deionized water (23 mL). Then, 0.25 mL of ammonia solution (25 wt%) was added into the solution. Followed by stirring for 1 h, 0.1 g tannic acid (TA) was added into the above solution. After TA dissolving completely, 0.19 mL of formaldehyde solution (37 wt%) was added and then continuously stirred for 24 h. Next, NiCl2 solution (0.05 g NiCl2 was dissolved in 1 mL of deionized water) was added into the above mixture and stirred for another 24 h. Subsequently, the products were transferred into an autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 100 °C for 24 h. Finally, the obtained colloidal Ni-PCS were collected by centrifugating and washing, and then re-dispersed in deionized water for further use.

0.5 mL of the as-prepared Ni-PCS solution was dispersed in 1.5 mL of deionized water containing 25 mg PVP. After stirring for 30 min, 2.4 mL of 0.3 mM H2PtCl6 solution was added and gently stirring for 10 min. Then, 0.125 mL of 3 mM NaBH4 was dropped into the above solution and reacted for 30 min. Followed by centrifugating at 12,000 rpm for 10 min and washing three times with ethanol, the final products of Pt nanoparticles functionalized Ni-phenolic coordination spheres (Pt@Ni-PCS) were obtained.

100 μL of 10 μg/mL detection anti-BALP (Ab2) was dropped into 1 mL of the above-prepared Pt@Ni-PCS solution and then gently stirred at 4 °C for 12 h. Based on Pt–N bond, the BALP antibody was assembled onto the surface of Pt@Ni-PCS composites. After blocking the nonspecific adsorption sites by BSA solution (0.5 wt%), the Pt@Ni-PCS-Ab2 bioconjugates were finally prepared and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The cytotoxicity of CDs and Zr-CD DRs was estimated by CCK-8 assay. MDA-MB-468 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate containing DMEM with 10% FBS and 5% CO2 at density of 5 × 104 cells/mL. After 24 h cultivation at 37 °C, the medium was replaced by 100 μL of DMEM containing different concentrations of CDs and Zr-CD DRs (10, 50, 100, and 500 μg/mL) and AuNPs (1 μg/mL), and then the cells were cultured for 48 h. Subsequently, 10 μL of CCK-8 reagent was dropped into each well and then incubated for 2 h at 37 °C to measure the optical density (OD) value at 450 nm with the multifunction microplate reader. Control experiments without CDs, Zr-CD DRs and AuNPs were performed under the same conditions. Each experiment was performed 3 times, and the data were averaged.

The glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Φ = 4 mm) was polished according to the previously reported work [16] to obtain clean electrode surface. Then, the Zr-CD DRs composites were dispersed in 0.1 wt% chitosan solution, which was further cast onto the clean surface of the pretreated GCE and then dried naturally to obtain the stable Zr-CD DRs/CS film. Then, 10 μL of AgNPs solution was dropped onto the surface of Zr-CD DRs/CS modified GCE and incubated at room temperature for 4 h. Subsequently, 10 μL of 10 μg/mL capture anti-BALP (Ab1) was droped onto the modified electrode and reacted at 4 °C for overnight. Next, 10 μL of 0.25 wt% BSA solution was dropped onto the electrode surface to hinder the nonspecific adsorption sites after incubation at 4 °C for 40 min, thus fabricating the ECL immunosensor (BSA/Ab1/AgNPs/Zr-CD DRs/CS/GCE).

ce:list-item.ce:para: μL of a series of different concentrations of BALP were dropped onto the fabricated ECL immunosensors and then incubated for 1 h. Subsequently, the modified electrodes were reacted with 10 μL of Pt@Ni-PCS-Ab2 bioconjugates for 1 h. At each step, the electrodes were rinsed with PBS to eliminate the unbound substances. Ultimately, the immunosensors were measured in 2 mL PBS containing 35 mM TEA under the potential ranging from 0.2 to 0.8 V at 0.2 V/s scan rate with 800 V PMT voltage.

ce:section.ce:para: The morphology characterization of as-synthesized CDs (the synthesis schematic diagram shown in Fig. 1 A) was explored by high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM). As displayed in Fig. 1B, HRTEM image exhibited monodispersed particles with the diameter of about 3 nm, indicating the as-synthesized CDs with good dispersity and homogeneity. Moreover, AFM image showed that CDs were uniform quasi-spherical structure and possessed an average height of about 3 nm (Fig. 1C). In addition, the optical property of CDs was also investigated and the results were exhibited in Fig. 1D. It showed that FL maximum excitation wavelength and maximum emission wavelength of the as-prepared CDs were 416 nm and 481 nm, respectively.

In order to confirm the successful synthesis of Zr-CD DRs, SEM was used to characterize the morphology of Zr-CD DRs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the morphology of Zr-CD DRs was the irregular multilayer coral-like structures. Additionally, the structure of the prepared Zr-CD DRs was also characterized by TEM (Fig. 2B). And TEM image present a multilayer coral, which was as the same as the SEM image.

The chemical composition of Zr-CD DRs was further investigated by high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) images and element mapping analysis. As shown in Fig. 2C–H, the C, N, O, Zr elements were evenly distributed onto the entire structure, indicating that the CDs were enriched by zirconium oxygen cluster with the coordination interaction, as expected. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental analysis was also employed. As described in Fig. S1, the C, N, O, Zr element peaks were observed, indicated that Zr-CD DRs were successfully synthesized by zirconium oxygen cluster aggregating CDs.

Moreover, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy characterization for structural analysis of Zr-CD DRs was also performed. As shown in Fig. 3 A, ZrOCl2 had no specific absorption peaks. Evidently, the UV–vis adsorption spectrum of CDs showed a typical adsorption band in the range of 200–250 nm assigned to π-π* transition, revealing the aromatic structure of the graphite cores [17]. Another two adsorption peaks could be observed at 326 nm and 396 nm, which were assigned to n-π* transitions, because abound groups contained lone electron pairs, such as CO, CN, C–OH and C–N existed on the CDs surface [18]. After coordination-induced self-assembly of CDs and Zr–O clusters to form Zr-CD DRs, the adsorption peaks assigned to n-π* transitions exhibited slightly redshifted due to the coordination interaction decreasing the π electron density, thus decreasing the energy level of π* orbital.

The crystal structures of the synthesized Zr-CD DRs were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD). Fig. 3B showed a typical wide-angle XRD pattern of CDs and Zr-CD DRs. The pattern clearly described an intense diffraction peak at approximately 25° 2θ, which was indexed to the C(002) plane [19]. The weak XRD peaks at approximately 43° and 55° 2θ were assigned to the C(100) and C(004) plane, respectively [20]. The entire XRD patterns revealed the moderate graphitic structure of CDs from Zr-CD DRs.

The structure of Zr-CD DRs was further investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to elucidate the bonding states of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and zirconium (Fig. 3C). By further analysis of the C1s band, four peaks at 284.5, 285.3, 286.8 and 287.9 eV were found in Fig. 3D, which should be attributed to C–C/CC, C–N, C–O, and CO bands, respectively. The characteristic peak at 284.5 eV indicated that sp2 had an aromatic or graphite structure. Moreover, the appearance of C–O and CO confirmed the existence of carboxyl and carbonyl groups in Zr-CD DRs. As shown in Fig. 3E, the N1s spectrum presented pyridinic N at 399.5 eV, pyrrolic N at 400.5 eV and graphitic N at 401.5 eV. The O1s spectrum presented three peaks at 531.5, 533.0, and 530.6 eV (Fig. 3F), which were attributed to CO, C–O, and Zr–O bands, respectively. The XPS spectrum of Zr3d showed a doublet of Zr3d5/2 and Zr3d3/2 at 182.4 and 184.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 3G). XPS data confirmed that nitrogen doped CDs with the carboxyl groups were enriched by zirconium oxygen cluster via the coordination interaction.

The morphologies of Ni-PCS and Pt@Ni-PCS were analysed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 4 A, Ni-PCS presented a uniform spherical morphology with a diameter of about 500 nm and the spherical surface was rough. After in situ synthesis of Pt nanoparticles on Ni-PCS surface, the Pt nanoparticles with a diameter of about 5 nm were uniform distribution on the surface of the sphere (Fig. 4B and C). As depicted in Fig. 4D–I, the HAADF-STEM images and element mapping results of Pt@Ni-PCS revealed that C, O, Ni and Pt components were uniformly distributed in the coordination spheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of CDs and Zr-CD DRs
ce:section.ce:para: Biocompatibility is an important parameter to assess the suitability of biomaterials in biomedical applications. Herein, to evaluate the feasibility of CDs and Zr-CD DRs as good biomedical materials, the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared CDs and Zr-CD DRs against the cultured MDA-MB-468 cells was explored by CCK-8 assay. Fig. 5 displayed the MDA-MB-468 cells viability after the incubation with the different concentrations of CDs and Zr-CD DRs samples (10, 50, 100, and 500 μg/mL). It could been seen that cell viability of Zr-CD DRs was significantly higher than that of pure CDs in different concentrations groups. More importantly, after incubation with 100 μg/mL of Zr-CD DRs for 48 h, about 75% of MDA-MB-468 cells were still alive, which was higher than that of 1.0 μg/mL Au NPs, demonstrating the low cytotoxicity of Zr-CD DRs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL quantum efficiency of Zr-CD DRs
ce:section.ce:para:
The ECL quantum efficiency is a key parameter to assess the ECL performance of luminophors. To prove that coordination-induced self-assembly could improve the ECL efficiency of CDs, the relative ECL quantum efficiencies of Zr-CD DRs to CDs were calculated by investigating the linear relationship between the ECL intensities and the currents of CDs (Fig. 6 A) and Zr-CD DRs (Fig. 6B), respectively. These results illustrated that the ECL intensity was proportional to the Faradaic current in the ECL system. According to the following equation [13]:
(1)

The quantitative relationship between ECL intensity and current in CDs and Zr-CD DRs system could be generalized into the following equations:

n , i F, t and k represent the ECL intensity, non-Faradaic current, Faradaic current, time and the slope of the linear response curve. Thus, the quotient (Q) of ⌀CDs and ⌀Zr-CD DRs could be calculated according to the following equation:

It could be seen that the ECL quantum efficiency of Zr-CD DRs showed 2.1-fold enhancement than that of CDs, indicating that the coordination interaction-enriched CDs by zirconium oxygen cluster was an effective way to enhance ECL quantum efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL and EIS characterization of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to effectively confirm the modification process of the electrode, ECL characterization was performed by detecting different modified electrodes in 2 mL PBS containing the co-reactant TEA. As curve a shown in Fig. 7 A, the bare electrode exhibited almost negligible ECL signal. When Zr-CD DRs/CS film was immobilized on the surface of the electrode, a strong ECL signal was achieved (curve b). After immediate incubation of AgNPs on the above-mentioned modified electrode, the ECL signal was further increased due to the good electrical conductivity of AgNPs (curve c). However, with continuous incubation with Ab1, BSA and target BALP on the modified electrode surface, the ECL signal decreased sequentially (curve d-f). Such a successive decrease was attributed that they were all non-electrically active substances to hinder the electrons transfer. As expected, the ECL signal decreased significantly by the introduction of Pt@Ni-PCS-Ab2 bioconjugates via sandwich immunoreaction (curve g). The reason was that Pt@Ni-PCS could efficiently quench the ECL signal of Zr-CD DRs/TEA system. These programmed ECL changes indicated that the ECL immunosensor was successfully fabricated.

To further evaluate the successful preparation of the ECL immunosensor in various steps, the EIS characterization was further employed and performed in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- containing 0.1 M KCl. As shown in Fig. 7B, the diameter of the semicircle represented the electron transfer resistance (Ret). Due to the free electron transfer process, the bare GCE present a small semicircular domain (curve a). When Zr-CD DRs/CS composites were coated onto the GCE, a large semicircular domain was observed (curve b), indicating that chitosan with poor conductivity hindered the electrons transfer. Smaller semicircular domain was observed after adsorbing Ag NPs on the surface of Zr-CD DRs/CS film (curve c) due to excellent electrical conductivity of Ag NPs. After continuously modifing with Ab1, BSA, BALP and Pt@Ni-PCS-Ab2 bioconjugates, Ret successively increased due to the inhibitory effect of proteins as the inert electron layer (curve d-g). Therefore, these results verified that the ECL immunosensor was successfully constructed, as expected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of immunosensor for BALP detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The analytical performance of the immunosensor was investigated under optimal reaction conditions. As displayed in Fig. 8 A, as target BALP concentrations increased ranging from 1 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL, the responding ECL signals gradually decreased. As the calibration plot displayed in Fig. 8B, it could be seen that the ECL signal was correlated with the logarithm of BALP concentration with a linear equation of I = −1122.75 lg c + 7385.08. And the correlation coefficient (R
2 ) of 0.9979 was obtained. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) of 24.9 fg/mL was calculated. Compared with the previous methods for BALP detection (Table S1), the developed ECL immunosensor showed a broader response range and lower limit of detection. This was ascribed to the following factors: (i) the as-prepared Zr-CD DRs/TEA system with excellent ECL performance provided a strong ECL signal for “signal on” state. (ii) The Pt@Ni–PCs composites could prominently quench the ECL signal of Zr-CD DRs/TEA system for “signal off” state.

To evaluate the specificity of the prepared ECL immunosensor, several interfering proteins such as alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), laminin (LN), prostate specific antigen (PSA) and human serum albumin (HSA) were selected. As shown in Fig. 8C, despite the concentration of interfering proteins up to 10 ng/mL, the ECL intensities of interferents kept at high ECL level as well as the blank group. In the presence of 1 ng/mL BALP concentration, the ECL intensity was significantly decreased. Besides, the ECL intensity of mixture containing 10 ng/mL interfering proteins (AFP, LN, PSA and HSA) and 1 ng/mL BALP was similar to that of only 1 ng/mL BALP. These results indicated the ECL immunosensor with excellent specificity, which could be attributed to the effective blocking of nonspecific sites by BSA and the highly specific recognition of antigens and antibodies.

Excellent stability was one of the main factor to expand the clinical application of the ECL immunosensor. Fig. 8D showed the ECL intensities of the immunosensor for 10 pg/mL and 10 ng/mL BALP detection by scanning continuously for 10 cycles, respectively. The ECL intensities were relatively stable with the RSD of 2.52% and 0.92%, indicating that the ECL immunosensor had a good stability.

To evaluate the feasibility of ECL immunosensor for clinical application, recovery test of BALP detection was performed in human serum samples by standard addition method. ELISA was also used to detect the above human serum samples containing different BALP concentrations to compare with the detection results of developed ECL immunosensor (Table S2). Due to the linear range of ELISA ranging from 6.25 ng/mL to 200 ng/mL, the low concentrations of BALP (0.500 ng/mL and 1.00 ng/mL) were not detected. However, no significant differences between two methods were obtained in the determination of the other three concentrations of BALP (5.00 ng/mL, 10.0 ng/mL, 50.0 ng/mL), demonstrating the reliability and practicability of the developed ECL immunosensor for BALP detection in real biological samples.

In summary, a strategy of coordination-induced self-assembly of carbon dots and zirconium oxygen clusters was first proposed to synthesize efficient ECL nanocomposites of Zr-CD DRs. By this way, the ECL efficiency of Zr-CD DRs was realized 2-fold enhancement than that of discrete CDs solution. Based on the Zr-CD DRs as the ECL signal labels and Pt@Ni-PCS as quenching labels, a highly sensitive “on-off” ECL immunosensor was successfully constructed for BALP detection. Comfortingly, the prepared immunosensing method exhibited a wide detection linear range with outstanding specificity and stability and clinical practicability, providing a promising tool for clinical detection of BALP. Importantly, coordination-induced self-assembly based Zr-CD DRs not only possess excellent ECL performance, but also has good biocompatibility, which holds great promise in ECL biosensing and bioimaging applications.

Zi Lin: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology. Wenqian Cheng: Formal analysis, Investigation. Changjin Liu: Investigation, Visualization. Min Zhao: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Shijia Ding: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Zhongliang Deng: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82272431, 21804015), the Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC (cstc2018jcyjAX0206).
